farRightText:
Actress Kaylee Hottle — best known for her role as Jia in two Godzilla films — died on Tuesday at the age of 19.

The actress’ father, Joshua Hottle, told TMZ his daughter was killed in a car accident in Maryland during the early hours of Tuesday morning.

Hottle’s father also posted a nearly 23-minute-long video to social media in which he revealed via American Sign Language that his daughter had died, adding that he had to fly from Texas to claim her body.

“I am taking a flight that I never would like to take,” Joshua Hottle wrote in the caption of his post.

Watch Below:

I am taking a flight that I never would like to take. Posted by Joshua Hottle on Tuesday, July 21, 2026

In the video, the actress’ father explains that he learned Hottle was involved in a serious car accident, and that shortly after receiving this news, officials had called to inform him her heart stopped on the way to the hospital.

The 19-year-old actress — born in Atlanta, Georgia — came from a multi-generational deaf family, and was also deaf herself.

She is best known for her roles as Jia — a teenager who communicates with King Kong through American Sign Language — in the 2021 film, Godzilla vs. Kong, and in the 2024 movie, Godzilla X Kong: The New Empire.

Hottle, who got her start in commercials, also appeared as Joon on one episode of the TV series, Magnum P.I..

At just 9 years old, the actress appeared in a Public Service Announcement for the live video messaging app Glide, which is used by many members of the deaf and hard-of-hearing community.

The Frederick County Sheriff’s Office said deputies and Fire and Rescue Services responded to the car crash around 2:52 a.m. on Tuesday, according to a report by CBS News.

A preliminary investigation reportedly found that a 1995 Honda Accord driven by a 19-year-old man traveled off a road and struck a culvert. Investigators added that they believe excessive speed was a contributing factor in the crash.

The driver was taken to a hospital with non-life-threatening injuries, while a third person who was in the vehicle declined medical services at the scene of the car accident.

Alana Mastrangelo is a reporter for Breitbart News. You can follow her on Facebook and X at @ARmastrangelo, and on Instagram.

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump on Monday imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, declaring that Canada has unfairly discriminated against American autos, alcohol and dairy products. The move could unleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House. The administration official previewing the action said that Canada was one of the only nations other than China that retaliated against Trump’s previous tariffs and must be held accountable. Advertisement The official insisted on anonymity on a call with reporters to preview the president’s actions and said that Trump signed three proclamations to launch the tariffs under Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section because they said Trump could use it to destabilize the economy. The new 50% tariffs would exclude energy products, potash, fish and critical minerals, but they would include goods that had previously been protected from import taxes by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA. That 2020 trade pact was not renewed by the U.S., triggering a new set of negotiations that could run until 2036. The White House said in a fact sheet that the tariffs would go into effect in 30 days, meaning there is time for negotiations as Trump has not always followed through on his announced tax hikes on imports. Advertisement Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said in a statement that his government believes in the “benefits of free and fair trade,” having signed “more than 20 new economic and security partnerships.” He said Canada is prepared to negotiate with the Trump administration. “This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the U.S.,” Carney said. “Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the U.S. to the mutual benefit of our citizens.” U.S. President Donald Trump, left, speaks with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, right, at a working lunch with leaders of G7 and the Middle East, in Evian-les-Bains, France, on June 16, 2026. Evelyn Hockstein/Pool Photo via AP Advertisement Canada Faces The Risk Of A Broader Trade War Still, the tariffs could escalate into a wider trade war as Canada seeks to defend its economy. Ontario Premier Doug Ford saw a possible showdown ahead. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ford posted on social media. Candace Laing, CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, said the Trump administration’s moves were “regrettable” but the two countries need to use the 30-day window before the tariffs start “to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks.” Advertisement Chris Swonger, CEO of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, also called for a deal: “We encourage policymakers on both sides of the border to pursue a negotiated solution that restores market access for U.S. spirits and avoids further harm to the U.S. hospitality sector.” But the use of a Great Depression-era law to impose the tariffs broadens some of the risks, as those tariffs could be applied to other U.S. trading partners, not just Canada, and inject “massive uncertainty” into the global economy, said Scott Lincicome, vice president of general economics at the Cato Institute, a libertarian think tank. “We crossed the Rubicon,” Lincicome said. “The invocation of 338 is the nuclear option for Trump tariffs.” Advertisement Tariffs Are Also A Political Challenge For Trump The new tariffs carry serious political and economic risks for Trump ahead of the November midterm elections for control of Congress. His “Liberation Day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. President Donald Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. (AP Photo/Mark Schiefelbein, File) AP Photo/Mark Schiefelbein The Supreme Court ruled this February that Trump had lacked the legal authority to impose the tariffs by declaring an economic emergency, causing the administration to find alternative ways to raise import taxes based on a series of legal authorities. Advertisement Tariffs are taxes on imports, which companies can then pass along to consumers in the form of higher prices. The president maintains that the costs created by tariffs will cause manufacturing to relocate to the U.S., though there is little evidence of that in the economic data. “These new taxes will raise prices on American families and likely lead to retaliation against the very industries Trump purportedly wants to protect,” said Rep. Suzan DelBene, D-Wash., who is chair of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. The latest import taxes could worsen Trump’s weak ratings on the economy. He promised voters when running for the presidency that he would bring prices down, but the annual inflation rate has risen since he became president because the tariffs and the war in Iran are pushing up oil prices. Advertisement Trump Has Repeatedly Targeted Canada On Trade Issues The Trump administration official said the president had also requested that his aides look into additional tariffs on Canada because its wildfires hurt air quality in the U.S. He had publicly threatened to do so in social media posts. At the World Cup final on Sunday, Trump watched the game with Carney. The Trump administration official said their time together at the game was not a working visit to discuss trade and tariffs. Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after the U.S. president imposed tariffs on Canada under the pretext that it should do more to stop fentanyl smuggling. Advertisement Trump noted in his autos proclamation that Canada maintained, starting in April 2025, a 25% tariff on the imports of U.S. motor vehicles that did not qualify for preferential treatment under the USMCA. The White House said that, regarding alcohol, all but two Canadian provinces and territories halted the purchase and retailing of American alcoholic beverages beginning last year, which was also a response to Trump’s tariffs and taunts of making Canada the 51st state. But Trump has long objected to Canada’s treatment of U.S. cheese, saying in his proclamation that Canada discriminates against the U.S. compared to Europe on dairy products. Advertisement Trump and Carney have had a frosty relationship, with Carney, a former central banker, who pledged to go “elbows up” for Canada during his election campaign last year. At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, in January, Carney called out Trump — without naming him — by saying that the “most powerful” countries are using the economy to coerce less powerful nations. Trump responded at the time by saying: “Canada lives because of the United States.” ___ Associated Press writer Jim Morris contributed from Vancouver, British Columbia.

topic: Army Veteran Fire Outside Federal Building

right-center.title: Army veteran faces arson charge after fire attack outside NYC federal building

centerText: A former US Army soldier has been arrested after igniting a fire in front of a federal building in New York City, says the FBI. Andrew Arrabaca, 43, allegedly shot a pellet gun and set off fireworks on Monday morning at 26 Federal Plaza, where the FBI, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), and US Citizenship and Immigration Services have offices. Federal agents tackled the suspect, who they say was armed with a machete and other weapons. Three people sustained minor injuries in the incident, including an immigrant who was attending a status hearing, said the FBI. The lower Manhattan site has become a flashpoint for protests against President Donald Trump's immigration policies. The suspect, whom the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) called an "anti-ICE rioter", launched fireworks before pouring an accelerant on the stairs of an entrance to the building, causing a fire. The BBC has contacted DHS for comment. The fire department put out the blaze. Arrabaca "made several anti-government and anti-ICE statements during the incident", the FBI said. FBI New York Assistant Director James Barnacle said during a news conference that Arrabaca was armed with two axes, a machete, three knives and fireworks. He fired about five to seven pellets from an airsoft gun in the direction of the building, Barnacle said. Of those injured, two were government employees, Barnacle said. DHS said a bystander was grazed, and a security officer who tackled and detained Arrabaca sustained minor injuries. "He is an anti-American, anti-government extremist," Barnacle added. Arrabaca, of Poughkeepsie, New York state, served in the regular Army from 2001-04 and in the Army National Guard-New York from 2004-05, according to the BBC's US partner CBS. He did not have any deployments and left the Army with the rank of specialist. New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani called the incident "deeply disturbing" in a post on X. "Our administration will continue to ensure that every New Yorker is safe in their city - and hold accountable any who threaten that," he said.
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! The U.S. Army veteran accused of setting a fire outside a New York City federal building that houses an immigration court and other government agencies, including the FBI, and firing a pellet gun, will face an arson charge related to the Monday attack, which he had been planning for months, federal prosecutors said. Andrew Arrabaca, 43, appeared in a Manhattan federal court Tuesday for his alleged actions outside 26 Federal Plaza that injured three people before he was subdued. He did not enter a plea. Despite only a few injuries, U.S. Magistrate Judge Robyn F. Tarnofsky, noted that the attack could have ended tragically. "The crime you’re charged with is troubling," said Tarnofsky, who immediately conducted a bail hearing and ordered Arrabaca to be released to a mental health facility. Arrabaca, who served as a mechanic on Patriot missile systems in the Army, harbored anti-ICE and anti-government views, James Barnacle, assistant director in charge of the FBI's New York Field Office, said Monday. FBI ARRESTS PROTESTER WHO THREATENED TO KILL ICE OFFICER'S FAMILY AT NJ DETENTION CENTER PROTEST, BLANCHE SAYS "He is an anti-American, anti-government extremist," Barnacle told reporters at the scene. Voter records show he has registered as a Democrat and was a member of the left-wing Working Families party. Arrabaca "came armed with bad intentions" and was found with a range of weapons, including a pellet gun, knives, a hammer, and a machete. Authorities said the upstate New York resident arrived at the federal building sometime around 8:30 a.m. and lit a large firework before pouring a liquid that ignited a fire. Videos and images from the scene showed a fire with a plume of smoke and bright orange flames near a building entrance, as well as law enforcement officers restraining the suspect. VIDEO SHOWS MOMENT CHICAGO MAN ALLEGEDLY USED SUV AS WEAPON AGAINST ICE AGENT, PROSECUTORS SAY In addition to allegedly setting the fire, Arrabaca also fired five to seven pellets from an airsoft rifle toward the building, Barnacle said. He was arrested by federal officials a short time later and has been in custody since. He made several anti-government and anti-ICE statements during the incident and said "he was okay if he hurt or killed people going in and out of the building, whether they were federal employees or civilians," the FBI said. Upon his arrest, authorities said they found Arrabaca with two axes, a hammer, a machete and three knives. Federal prosecutors said he had been planning the attack since January, when he began buying supplies and visiting sites around New York. Arrabaca allegedly told law enforcement that he went to several other sites around the city bringing materials and considered attacking them. After months, he decided to attack the federal building near the courthouse. On Sunday, he made his way to Lower Manhattan with the weapons and other items used during the attack, prosecutors allege. He spent the night in a park and arrived at the building on Monday morning. He told law enforcement he targeted a specific entrance because it was busy and employees were heading to work and members of the public were heading inside. Arrabaca admitted that he was aware people could be hurt and was willing to accept the risk, said prosecutors, who described his actions as "calculated." He was also aware he could die. Defense lawyer Jennifer Brown said Arrabaca has mental health issues, and asked that he be placed in a Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) mental health hospital for evaluation and treatment. Prosecutors noted that Arrabaca does not have a stable residence or employment and was living out of a car before the attack. "The mental health issues that are quite likely the cause of the behavior, I seriously considered the alternatives proposed by Ms. Brown, but I had to reject them because I don’t think it’s sufficiently clear you would comply with treatment," Tarnofsky told Arrabaca. The federal building in Lower Manhattan has been the scene of protests against U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the agency charged with enforcing the nation's immigration laws amid the Trump administration's crackdown on illegal immigration. A preliminary hearing is scheduled for Aug. 4. He faces a mandatory minimum of seven years in prison. Fox News Digital's Michael Ruiz contributed to this report.

centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! Smithsonian National Museum of American History Director Anthea Hartig appeared stumped by a basic question on sex and gender from Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C. During a House hearing on alleged "extreme political activism" on display at the Smithsonian, Mace asked Hartig, "Do you believe that men can become women?" Hartig hesitated before answering, "Ma'am, as the director of the museum, that's not relevant in this case." "It's very relevant," Mace shot back. Motioning to images of Smithsonian displays featuring drag queens, Mace continued, "You've got men dressed up as women, and you have them on displays in front of kids. Do you think men can become women? Yes or no?" DISCRIMINATORY 'SHADOW' REVIEW PROCESS LOCKS WHITE, ASIAN STUDENTS OUT OF CALIFORNIA MED SCHOOL, DOJ ALLEGES Hartig did not answer the question but instead began to discuss one of the drag queen displays pictured on the board behind Mace. The congresswoman interrupted her. "What is a woman, Dr. Hartig?" Mace demanded. Hartig began to answer that "as a director and as a historian, it's not my-" before Mace cut her off again, saying, "You sexualized the American History Museum. So, this is a fair question. What is a woman?" Looking down for a moment, Hartig responded, "We strive to represent and not categorize all Americans." "OK, I have another question for you," Mace continued. "There's a glossary at the museum that informs visitors that while American culture traditionally recognizes two genders, other countries recognize three or more." "So, enlighten me this morning: how many genders are there?" Mace asked. Hartig hesitated again before asking, "Ma’am, which glossary?" "This is a display, and it has a glossary that says other countries recognize three or more genders. So, I would like you to enlighten me today; you're the director of the American History Museum, who's sexualized things in front of kids. I want to know, I want to ask you, how many genders are there?" Finally, Hartig responded, "I'm not sure which glossary you're speaking," before adding, "I’m happy to get back to you about that glossary." ANTI-ICE WALKOUT EXPOSES HOW SCHOOL DISTRICT 'FAILED' PARENTS, RISKED STUDENT SAFETY: 'BROKEN MY TRUST' Mace pressed further, saying, "One of your exhibits at the American History Museum says sometimes gender isn't revealed at birth. So, when is gender revealed?" "When was your gender revealed to you, Dr. Hartig?" she added. Smiling, Hartig simply answered, "I don't really think that's relevant to the conversation." "It is relevant. You have a display at the American History Museum talking about, ‘Gender isn't always revealed at birth.’ So, when was your gender revealed?" insisted Mace. Hartig began to answer by saying, "So, since I am a Ph.D.-" before Mace yielded the floor back to the committee chair. Earlier this month, a sweeping new White House report concluded that the Smithsonian Institution's National Museum of American History has become a taxpayer-backed institution of "ideological capture" and "extreme political activism." According to the 162-page "Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History" report, delivered on July 4, 2026, the museum treats the American story as a political tool rather than a shared national inheritance. "The report concludes that the Smithsonian Institution, and the National Museum of American History in particular, under its current leadership and current interpretive ideology, cannot be trusted to tell America's story honestly and in a way that is inspiring, unifying, and worthy of our great republic," the White House Domestic Policy Council wrote in its July 4 report following up on President Donald Trump’s March 27 executive order. "By the intention and at the direction of current Museum and Smithsonian leadership, NMAH has become subject to institutional capture by a radical, activist ideology that is fundamentally opposed to telling the noble, honest story of the great country we know and love." FAR-LEFT ACTIVIST CONFERENCE AT TEACHERS UNION HQ RAISES CLASSROOM CONCERNS "At the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History, visitors young and old should encounter the story of the United States told with honesty, seriousness, and pride," the report's executive summary began. "In particular, it should help the American people understand where America came from, what makes it distinctive, and why it is worth preserving." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Hartig said in her opening statement that "the Smithsonian takes the concerns raised in the White House Domestic Policy Council's report seriously, and as we continue to fact-check the report, we unwaveringly attest that it does not fairly or accurately characterize the full body of work at the museum." SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER She asserted that "the museum does not take sides in America's political debates," but instead, "We preserve and document the evidence of American life in all of its breadth, so that the public can encounter the past and draw their own conclusions." "There is always room for improvement, but I also know the beauty and the inspiration and the expertise that lies in our collections, our exhibits, and our programming," said Hartig. Fox News Digital reached out to the Smithsonian Institution for additional comment. Fox News Digital’s Eric Mack contributed to this report.
farRightText: On Tuesday, the House Oversight Subcommittee on Delivering on Government Efficiency (DOGE) held a hearing on "Rewriting American History: Examining the Smithsonian’s Efforts to Reshape the Past," which followed a detailed 162-page PDF report issued by the White House Domestic Policy Council on July 4th that documented the wokeification of the Smithsonian National Museum of American History (NMAH) on an epic scale. Advertisement During the hearing, Republican Rep. Brandon Gill (TX-26) grilled Dr. Anthea Hartig, the director of the museum, on issues including a "Mass Action Tool Kit" that had been promoted by the museum under her leadership. As my RedState colleague Bob Hoge might say, it wasn't even a fair fight, as Hartig froze at various points during the questioning, feigned ignorance at other points, and seemed more keen on writing down notes on a notepad instead of directly answering Gill's questions. DIVE DEEPER: Smithsonian Museum of History Has Become 'Prime Tool of Social Justice' at Taxpayer Expense White House Calls Out Smithsonian for Ruining Pop Culture Exhibits With 'Divisive Political Narratives' Here's the highlight reel: I asked the Director of the Smithsonian's National Museum of American History why her organization promoted a "MASS Action Toolkit" that claimed "objectivity" is an aspect of "white supremacy culture." pic.twitter.com/XXKZED5ivy — Brandon Gill (@realBrandonGill) July 21, 2026 The Smithsonian's "MASS Action Toolkit" said that politeness is a "defining aspect of white supremacy culture." I asked the Director of the National Museum of American History if that means she's a white supremacist. pic.twitter.com/VUrIeUEZWt — Brandon Gill (@realBrandonGill) July 21, 2026 The Smithsonian promoted an exhibit that claimed Mickey Mouse was racist. I asked the Director of the National American History Museum why... pic.twitter.com/oY7Z9l4b7D — Brandon Gill (@realBrandonGill) July 21, 2026 I asked the Director of the National Museum of American History if it was appropriate for the Smithsonian Institution to sell "Latina power" merchandise. pic.twitter.com/BjZg5xmIfS — Brandon Gill (@realBrandonGill) July 21, 2026 Advertisement Here are four key findings from the report the Domestic Policy Council released: NMAH Fails to Substantively Present America’s Founders and Founding NMAH Has “Problematized” the 250th Anniversary of America’s Founding NMAH Removed “American History” from Its Mission Statement to “Get Out of the ‘America First’ Mentality” NMAH Has Abandoned Historical Scholarship for Political Activism They also accused the Smithsonian of “anti-white,” “illegal alien,” and “transgender activism,” and concluded there has been “ideological capture at the museum,” something Gill further exposed at the hearing. From the report: The serious concerns raised in this report are not about a few exhibits or a few controversial labels. They concern whether America’s premier history museum still presents America as a coherent nation and heritage worth teaching with gratitude and honesty. The Museum can broaden the story without dissolving the story. It can and should acknowledge slavery, injustice, mistreatment of Native peoples, and other grave wrongs without encouraging citizens to view their country mainly through suspicion, resentment, and division. But that is not what the National Museum of American History is doing. As it stands today, it would benefit most Americans, especially parents bringing their children for a tour, if the Smithsonian’s flagship history museum had a label at every entrance that reads: “Warning: the exhibits in this museum were prepared by people who don’t want you to love your country.” The findings of this report matter beyond the museum world. Not all Americans follow academic theories or curatorial fashions, but they do understand when a revered public institution has traded settled truths and basic history for political activism and division. They do notice when the heroes, principles, and events that formed the country are pushed to the margins. They notice when their children are introduced to inappropriate sexual and ideological content under the guise of civic education. And they object when their children and grandchildren are being indoctrinated to regard their country primarily as a source of oppression and unresolved grievance. All of this stems from actions President Donald Trump took in the first year of his second term in office.
